column17: Best for: Teams needing powerful database with spreadsheet-like interface. Best relational database in no-code space.
column18: Can get expensive with many users, formula language has learning curve, interface designer limited, mobile app is slow with large bases, API rate limits on free tier.
column19: TESTED: Built client project management system with linked records (clients, projects, tasks, time tracking). Relational capabilities were excellent (could pull project data into tasks via lookups). Automations handled notifications and status updates. However, once we hit 10 team members, the $20/user/month added up fast ($200/month). Mobile app was frustratingly slow with 10k+ records. Incredible for database power, expensive for large teams.

column15: Best for: Knowledge management + light database. Best all-in-one workspace for docs + databases.
column16: Not a true database (limited relational capabilities), automations are basic, slower with large datasets, no API access on free tier, offline support is limited.
column17: TESTED: Built company wiki + project tracker + CRM in Notion. The all-in-one approach was brilliant (docs, databases, wikis in one place). However, trying to do complex relationships (like Airtable) was frustrating. Automations were very basic compared to Airtable. At 50k+ rows, performance dropped noticeably. Perfect for knowledge management, Airtable is better for pure database power.

column14: Best for: Teams wanting documents + databases in one. Best "doc as app" builder.
column15: Steeper learning curve than Notion, smaller template library, automations not as powerful as Airtable, can get slow with large tables, pricing per-doc-creator is confusing.
column16: TESTED: Built product roadmap with embedded tables, status tracking, and automated notifications. The doc as app" approach was unique (everything in one document). Packs (integrations) were powerful but required some technical knowledge. However
column17: onboarding new team members took longer than Notion/Airtable due to steeper learning curve. Great if you love the doc-as-app concept
column18: Notion is easier for most teams."

column15: Best for: Enterprises migrating from Excel. Most spreadsheet-like with project management features.
column16: Feels outdated (UI is 2010s Excel-like), limited database features compared to Airtable, automations basic, expensive for advanced features, overkill for simple needs.
column17: TESTED: Used for project portfolio management. If you love Excel, you'll feel at home. Automations handled status changes and notifications well. However, the interface felt clunky compared to Airtable/Notion. Relational capabilities were weak (couldn't do complex lookups like Airtable). Perfect for Excel users who want something familiar, but Airtable is more modern and powerful.

column14: Best for: Project management with light database. Easiest to learn
column16: Not a true database (limited relational capabilities), automations can get expensive (charged per automation), overkill if you don't need project management features, UI can feel overwhelming.
column17: TESTED: Used for sales pipeline tracking. Visual interface was beautiful and very easy to learn (team was productive in 1 hour). However, trying to do complex relationships (like linking deals to tasks to contacts) was frustrating compared to Airtable. Automations were charged per recipe (added up fast). Perfect for project management with light data needs, Airtable is better for pure database work.
